book.title: Noise
book.author: Daniel Kahneman, Olivier Sibony, and Cass Sunstein

summaryBullets: Distinguishing noise from bias in judgment creates clearer judgment.
Judgment can fail through inconsistency as well as through systematic error.
This matters because decisions improve when people ask whether different decision makers would reach different answers on the same case.
A common mistake is treating every decision error as bias alone.
The pattern becomes easier to see when the average judgment looks acceptable while individual judgments vary wildly.
A practical starting move is to ask whether the same case gets the same answer, separate noise from bias, and measure variation across judges.
keyTakeaways: Distinguishing noise from bias in judgment creates clearer judgment. This perspective helps people make clearer decisions and follow through with more discipline when stakes rise.
practice: ask whether the same case gets the same answer
separate noise from bias
measure variation across judges
treat inconsistency as a problem to solve

summaryBlocks.text: Noise Is Different From Bias explains the main pattern behind the chapter in a direct and practical way. One core idea is this: Distinguishing noise from bias in judgment creates clearer judgment. Another key idea is this: Judgment can fail through inconsistency as well as through systematic error.

A useful starting move is to ask whether the same case gets the same answer, then separate noise from bias. Repeated use builds better judgment over time because small decisions start aligning with longer term goals.

summaryBullets: Seeing how time, mood, and context change judgments that should be steadier creates clearer judgment.
The same person can judge differently because the surrounding moment changed.
This matters because consistency matters because fairness and quality both suffer when context drives the answer.
A common mistake is assuming your own judgment is stable across changing conditions.
The pattern becomes easier to see when small shifts in time or pressure keep changing the call without good reason.
A practical starting move is to check whether the condition changed the judgment, delay high stakes calls in poor conditions, and use a written standard.

A practical starting move is to use rules for repeatable decisions, aggregate when possible, and compare the expert to the rule.
